original_abstract:
In many artificial intelligence applications, the ability to generate well-calibrated probabilistic predictions is just as vital as achieving high classification accuracy. While many powerful machine learning models excel at discrimination, their raw outputs often fail to reflect true probabilities. To bridge this gap, we introduce Bayesian Binning into Quantiles (BBQ), a new non-parametric calibration framework designed to overcome the rigidities of traditional binning and scaling techniques.

Functioning as a flexible post-processing step, BBQ can be seamlessly integrated with any binary classification algorithm. Unlike standard histogram binning, which relies on a single fixed partition, our approach employs Bayesian model averaging to combine multiple binning schemes. This allows the model to remain robust against poorly chosen bin widths and irregular data distributions. Experimental evaluations across a diverse range of real-world and simulated datasets demonstrate that BBQ is computationally efficient and consistently out-performs common benchmarks—such as Platt scaling and isotonic regression—in producing accurate, reliable probability estimates.
humanized_abstract:
Accurate probabilistic prediction is often as important as accurate classification in many AI applications. While many models are very discriminative they often produce outputs that are poorly suited for a probabilistic interpretation. Bayesian Binning into Quantiles (BBQ) is a novel non-parametric calibration approach that extends the benefits of binning and scaling methods while avoiding their main shortcomings.

BBQ is a highly flexible post-processing procedure that can be used in conjunction with any binary classification algorithm. Unlike standard histogram binning which divides the range of values into fixed-sized bins, the Bayesian BBQ method uses Bayesian model averaging to combine the predictions from multiple binning regimes, resulting in a more robust procedure that is less sensitive to the choice of bin width and that handles more unusual distributions in the data. Experiments are reported on a variety of real-world and simulated test sets for several different classification algorithms and show that BBQ yields accurate and reliable posterior probabilities very efficiently, significantly out-performing standard competitors such as Platt scaling and isotonic regression.
